params.code:
# FOUR WAYS TO SORT THE SAME BARS, and they all end in the same place. What differs is
# how much WORK they do to get there — and that difference is not a detail, it is the
# whole of what "an algorithm" means.
#
# Every algorithm below is written to record a frame at each COMPARISON: the array as it
# stands, and the two bars it is looking at. `step` scrubs through those frames, so the
# slider is not an animation of the code — it IS the code, stopped.
n = 12      #@param int min=6 max=20
algo = 0    #@param int min=0 max=3
step = 30   #@param int min=0 max=400
seed = 5    #@param int min=1 max=999
NAMES = ["bubble", "insertion", "selection", "quicksort"]

def _run(kind, a, rec=True):
    a = list(a)
    frames = []                                  # (snapshot, i, j) at every comparison
    tally = [0]
    def cmp(i, j):
        tally[0] += 1
        if rec:                                  # counting 20,000 comparisons is free;
            frames.append((list(a), i, j))       # photographing all of them is not
    if kind == 0:                                # BUBBLE: swap neighbours until nobody is
        for p in range(len(a)):                  # out of order. The dumbest thing that works.
            done = True
            for i in range(len(a) - 1 - p):
                cmp(i, i + 1)
                if a[i] > a[i + 1]:
                    a[i], a[i + 1] = a[i + 1], a[i]
                    done = False
            if done:
                break
    elif kind == 1:                              # INSERTION: how you sort a hand of cards.
        for i in range(1, len(a)):               # Fast when the data is nearly sorted already.
            j = i
            while j > 0:
                cmp(j - 1, j)
                if a[j - 1] <= a[j]:
                    break
                a[j - 1], a[j] = a[j], a[j - 1]
                j -= 1
    elif kind == 2:                              # SELECTION: find the smallest, put it first.
        for i in range(len(a)):                  # Always n^2/2 comparisons — it cannot get lucky.
            m = i
            for j in range(i + 1, len(a)):
                cmp(m, j)
                if a[j] < a[m]:
                    m = j
            a[i], a[m] = a[m], a[i]
    else:                                        # QUICKSORT: split around a pivot, and never
        stack = [(0, len(a) - 1)]                # compare across the split again. That is the
        while stack:                             # whole trick, and it is worth n^2 -> n log n.
            lo, hi = stack.pop()
            if lo >= hi:
                continue
            pivot, i = a[hi], lo
            for j in range(lo, hi):
                cmp(j, hi)
                if a[j] <= pivot:
                    a[i], a[j] = a[j], a[i]
                    i += 1
            a[i], a[hi] = a[hi], a[i]
            stack.extend([(lo, i - 1), (i + 1, hi)])
    frames.append((list(a), -1, -1))             # the last frame: sorted, nothing to look at
    return a, frames, tally[0]

rng = random.Random(int(seed))
start = [rng.randint(2, 26) for _ in range(int(n))]
costs = {k: _run(k, start, rec=False)[2] for k in range(4)}
# ...and the same four, on two hundred numbers. Nothing is drawn for these: they are here
# because the gap between n^2 and n log n does not become visible at twelve. It becomes
# visible at two hundred, and it becomes fatal long before a million.
big = [rng.randint(2, 999) for _ in range(200)]
costs200 = {k: _run(k, big, rec=False)[2] for k in range(4)}
final, frames, _ = _run(int(algo), start)
k = min(max(int(step), 0), len(frames) - 1)
arr, ai, aj = frames[k]
result = {"arr": arr, "i": ai, "j": aj, "k": k, "frames": len(frames) - 1,
          "costs": costs, "costs200": costs200, "name": NAMES[int(algo)], "algo": int(algo), "n": int(n),
          "done": k >= len(frames) - 1, "W": 108.0, "H": 2.4}

# THE TWO BARS IT IS LOOKING AT RIGHT NOW. Everything an algorithm does, it does by
# comparing two things — and this is the pair, at step k. Nothing else on the screen is
# under its eye at this instant.
D = in_0
result = [max(D["arr"][x] * D["H"], 0.3) for x in (D["i"], D["j"]) if x >= 0]

D = in_0
W, H = D["W"], D["H"]
cell = W / len(D["arr"])
result = [Vector(-W / 2 + (x + 0.5) * cell, 0, max(D["arr"][x] * H, 0.3) / 2)
          for x in (D["i"], D["j"]) if x >= 0]

# THE BILL. The same twelve numbers, sorted four ways, and the columns are how many
# comparisons each way needed. They all produce the identical answer. One of them does it
# for a fraction of the price, and the gap is not 20% — drag `n` and watch it become a
# chasm.
D = in_0
out = []
mx = max(D["costs"].values()) or 1
for a in range(4):
    h = max(D["costs"][a] / mx * 40.0, 0.4)
    out.append(Pos(72.0, -30 + a * 15.0, h / 2) * Box(9, 9, h))
result = out

# THE ARRAY, as it stands at step k. Last of the geometry on purpose: the "pair under the
# eye" above is legitimately empty on the final frame, when there is nothing left to
# compare, and __result__ is the last GEOMETRY node.
D = in_0
result = [max(v * D["H"], 0.3) for v in D["arr"]]

D = in_0
W, H = D["W"], D["H"]
cell = W / len(D["arr"])
result = [Vector(-W / 2 + (i + 0.5) * cell, 0, max(v * H, 0.3) / 2)
          for i, v in enumerate(D["arr"])]

D = in_0
NAMES = ["bubble", "insertion", "selection", "quicksort"]
c = D["costs"]
lines = [f"{D['name']}   step {D['k']} of {D['frames']}"
         + ("   — SORTED" if D["done"] else f"   (comparing {D['i']} and {D['j']})"),
         "comparisons on these " + str(D["n"]) + " numbers:"]
lines += [f"   {NAMES[a]:<10s} {c[a]:5d}" + ("   <-- you are here" if a == D["algo"] else "")
          for a in range(4)]
c2 = D["costs200"]
lines.append("")
lines.append("the same four, on 200 numbers (nothing is drawn — just counted):")
lines += [f"   {NAMES[a]:<10s} {c2[a]:7,d}" for a in range(4)]
lines.append(f"   ratio: {c2[0] / max(c2[3], 1):.0f}x  —  and it grows with every number "
             "you add")
result = "\n".join(lines)

params.text:
THE SAME ANSWER, AT FOUR DIFFERENT PRICES.

Twelve numbers, four algorithms, one slider. `step` (on the Code Block) scrubs through the run: every frame is one COMPARISON, with the two bars under the eye drawn in their own colour. That is all any of these algorithms ever does — look at two numbers and decide which is bigger. Everything else is bookkeeping.

Drag `step` from 0 to the end and watch the bars settle. Then switch `algo` and watch a completely different route to the identical destination:
0 BUBBLE — swap neighbours until nobody is out of order. The dumbest thing that works, and it takes forever.
1 INSERTION — how you sort a hand of cards. Nearly-sorted data barely costs it anything, which is why real sorting libraries fall back to it on small chunks.
2 SELECTION — find the smallest, put it first, repeat. It always costs n²/2 comparisons: it cannot get lucky, and it cannot get unlucky either.
3 QUICKSORT — split the array around a pivot, and never compare across the split again. That single refusal is worth the whole difference between n² and n log n.

The four columns on the right are the bill: how many comparisons each one needed on THESE twelve numbers. At twelve, honestly, nobody cares — bubble asks 66 and quicksort 40, and any of them would do. The Panel underneath quietly runs all four on TWO HUNDRED numbers instead, and there the polite little gap has become 19,900 against about 1,500. Add a zero to the input and the gap squares itself again.

That is what "n² versus n log n" means when it stops being a phrase in a textbook. It is not that the slow algorithm is slow. It is that it stops being an option at all, at a size the fast one has not even noticed.

The answer was never in doubt. Choosing how to get there is the entire discipline.
